a.xlink:title: Controls how raw text/code becomes token sequences presented to the model. Choices here shape distribution, context construction, and the effective learning signal under a fixed compute budget.
Determines the domains and genres the model observes during training. Source composition controls coverage, bias, and the kind of generalization the model can learn.
Removes low-quality, duplicated, or harmful text before training. Better filtering usually increases effective data quality without increasing parameters.
Maps text into discrete tokens that define the model's input/output alphabet. Token choices affect sequence lengths, embedding size, and downstream memory/parameter budgets.
Defines how tokens are packed into fixed-length training sequences. Packing strategy changes utilization, padding waste, and effective batch size under memory limits.
Determines how examples are selected and grouped into batches. Batching decisions affect gradient noise, throughput, and peak memory during training.
Applies transformations that change the training distribution without adding parameters. Augmentation can improve robustness but must preserve label semantics for next-token prediction.
Controls how different data types or difficulties are scheduled over training. Curricula can improve convergence and sample-efficiency without increasing parameters.
Defines the parameterized function that maps tokens to next-token probabilities. Under Parameter Golf, architecture choices must trade off accuracy against strict parameter and memory ceilings.
Maps discrete tokens into continuous vectors the network can process. Embeddings often dominate parameter count in tiny LLMs because they scale with vocabulary size.
Stacks attention and feed-forward blocks to model complex token dependencies. Under Parameter Golf, most innovation lives in reallocating parameter/memory across these sub-components.
Maps final hidden states to logits over the vocabulary. The output layer can match embedding size and may rival embeddings in parameter cost unless weights are tied or factorized.

Specifies how the model is optimized and scored, including loss design, optimizer state, precision, and benchmark harness constraints. These choices strongly affect peak memory and convergence at small scale.
Defines the training objective that shapes gradients. Loss modifications can change calibration and stability without adding parameters to the model.
Controls parameter updates and determines additional optimizer-state memory. Optimizer choice is a major contributor to peak training memory at fixed parameter counts.
Chooses numeric formats for weights, activations, and gradients. Lower precision reduces memory and bandwidth but can require stabilization tricks to train reliably.
Controls how intermediate activations are stored for backpropagation. Checkpointing can reduce peak memory by recomputing activations at the cost of extra compute.
Defines how the model is scored and constrained, including exact benchmark implementation. A stable harness is required to compare innovations under the same Parameter Golf rules.
Tracks parameter count and memory usage to enforce constraints and guide component swaps. Accurate accounting enables deliberate tradeoffs rather than accidental OOM failures.
Broad natural-language text from the web provides diverse topics and writing styles. It tends to increase general knowledge but can introduce noise and duplication.
Programming language corpora emphasize syntax, long-range dependencies, and precise token patterns. Code data often improves reasoning-like behaviors at small model sizes.
Long-form text increases exposure to extended discourse structure and narrative coherence. It can help models learn longer-context patterns even with limited context windows.
Synthetic data can target specific skills or failure modes by generating curated examples. It risks distribution shift or error amplification if generation quality is low.
Detects and removes near-duplicate documents or passages to reduce memorization and wasted steps. This increases data efficiency at fixed compute.
Keeps only target languages (or mixes them deliberately) to control distribution. It reduces accidental multilingual drift in a tiny model.
Uses heuristics or learned scoring to prioritize coherent, information-dense text. It effectively increases signal-to-noise ratio without changing architecture.
Removes or down-weights content associated with harmful or toxic language. It improves behavioral robustness with negligible model-side cost.
Byte Pair Encoding learns a subword vocabulary that balances token count and coverage. It is a standard choice for compact tokenization at scale.
Unigram tokenization chooses tokens via a probabilistic model, often yielding more flexible segmentations. It can reduce brittleness to rare substrings.
Operates on bytes before merging into subwords, ensuring full coverage of arbitrary text. It often increases sequence length slightly but avoids unknown tokens.
Uses characters as tokens, guaranteeing coverage but producing much longer sequences. This typically increases activation/KV-cache memory for a fixed context length.
Controls how multiple documents are concatenated or separated to fill context windows. Better packing increases tokens-per-step at the same memory cost.
Sets the maximum number of tokens processed per forward pass. Memory scales roughly linearly for activations and KV cache, making this a key Parameter Golf lever.
Concatenates samples into fixed-size blocks with padding where needed. Simple to implement but can waste compute on padding tokens.
Dynamically packs variable-length samples to minimize padding and maximize real tokens per batch. This improves throughput under strict memory budgets.
Short context reduces activation and KV-cache memory, enabling larger batch sizes or wider models. It can hurt tasks requiring long-range dependencies.
Moderate context is a common tradeoff between long-range modeling and memory cost. It keeps KV cache manageable while retaining some long-context capacity.
Long context increases both activation memory and KV cache substantially. This often forces reductions in model width/depth under a fixed memory ceiling.
Samples sequences uniformly or with simple weights, producing diverse batches. It is robust but can incur padding inefficiency with variable lengths.
Groups sequences of similar length to minimize padding and wasted compute. This increases effective tokens-per-step under a fixed memory budget.
Randomly masks contiguous spans and trains the model to predict missing tokens. This can encourage modeling longer dependencies and reduce overfitting.
Randomly drops or replaces tokens during training to discourage reliance on brittle cues. It is a lightweight way to increase robustness at tiny scale.
Creates multiple cropped views of the same document to encourage consistent representations. It can help compact models learn invariances but adds pipeline complexity.
Starts with shorter sequences and gradually increases context length. This can stabilize early training and manage memory usage over time.
Adjusts sampling probabilities across corpora to control the effective training distribution. Mixing is a powerful lever for tiny models where capacity is limited.
Defines how token IDs are converted to vectors and how much parameter budget is allocated to that mapping. Changing this is a common way to free parameters for the backbone.
Injects token position information so the model can distinguish order. Some methods are parameter-free but affect attention behavior and memory scaling with context length.
A learned lookup table mapping each vocabulary token to a dense vector. It is expressive but can consume a large fraction of the parameter budget.
Represents embeddings as a low-rank factorization to reduce parameters while preserving expressivity. This trades some embedding capacity for parameter headroom.
Maps multiple tokens to shared embedding slots via hashing or sharing rules to reduce parameters. This can free significant budget but may introduce collisions that hurt accuracy.
Applies a deterministic rotation to query/key vectors based on position, enabling extrapolation to longer contexts. It is parameter-free and common in modern LLMs.
Adds a head-dependent linear bias to attention logits that encodes relative positions. It is parameter-free and can improve long-context generalization under budget.
Uses a learned embedding for each position up to the context limit. It adds parameters proportional to context length and can be less robust to extrapolation.
A repeated unit combining attention, MLP, normalization, and residual connections. Its internal design determines both parameter efficiency and activation/KV-cache memory.
Computes content-dependent mixing of token representations to model long-range interactions. Attention is memory-heavy due to attention logits/activations and KV cache during inference.
Applies per-token nonlinear transformations that expand and contract hidden representations. MLP layers often dominate parameter count and a large share of activation memory.
Stabilizes activations and gradients to enable deep training. Normalization has low parameter cost but strongly affects optimization dynamics in small models.
Adds skip connections that improve gradient flow and allow iterative refinement of representations. Residuals add negligible parameters but contribute to activation storage.
Randomly zeroes activations during training to reduce overfitting. It has no parameters but can change optimization behavior and effective capacity in tiny models.
Linear layers that produce queries, keys, values, and output projections. Projection parameter count scales roughly with hidden size squared, making it a major budget lever.
Computes attention weights and applies them to values. The choice of implementation affects peak activation memory and speed, especially at longer context lengths.
Stores past keys/values for autoregressive decoding to avoid recomputation. KV cache memory scales with context length and head dimensions, often dominating inference memory.
Projects hidden states into query vectors used to retrieve information from keys/values. Its parameters scale with hidden size and number of layers.
Projects hidden states into key vectors that index information in attention. Key projection can be per-head (more parameters) or shared across heads (fewer parameters).
Projects hidden states into value vectors that carry content aggregated by attention weights. Like keys, values can be per-head or shared to reduce parameters and KV cache.
Projects concatenated head outputs back to the model hidden size. It is required for standard multi-head attention and scales with hidden size squared.
Computes distinct key vectors for each attention head, maximizing expressivity. This typically uses more parameters than shared-key variants.
Shares key projections across heads or head groups, reducing parameter count. It often trades some expressivity for large memory savings in KV cache during inference.
Computes distinct value vectors per head to maximize representational capacity. This uses more parameters and increases KV cache size compared to shared-value variants.
Shares value projections across heads or head groups, reducing parameter count. It can significantly reduce inference KV cache memory at small parameter budgets.
Determines how attention logits and softmax are computed and stored. Memory-efficient implementations can reduce peak activation memory without changing parameters.
Materializes attention logits/weights more directly, increasing activation memory at long context. It is widely supported but often memory-limited in Parameter Golf settings.
Computes attention in tiles to avoid storing full attention matrices. This can sharply reduce peak activation memory while preserving standard softmax attention.
Does not store past keys/values, recomputing them each step. This reduces memory but increases compute and can be infeasible for long contexts.
Stores keys/values in half precision, balancing fidelity and memory usage. This is a common baseline for inference memory accounting.
Quantizes KV cache to 8-bit representations to reduce memory footprint. This can introduce approximation error but may be critical under strict memory budgets.
Chooses the internal structure of the MLP (activation, gating, sparsity, or factorization). This is a prime target for parameter reallocation toward OOD ideas.
Standard two-layer feed-forward network with a pointwise nonlinearity. It is a strong baseline but uses many parameters for a given hidden size.
Uses a gated activation that can improve quality at fixed depth. It often increases parameters slightly versus a dense FFN but can be more compute-efficient per gain.
Factorizes large MLP matrices into low-rank components to reduce parameters. This can free budget for attention or new modules but may bottleneck capacity.
Routes tokens to a subset of expert MLPs, increasing total parameters without proportional compute. It can boost capacity but complicates routing and memory accounting.
Normalizes activations using mean and variance across hidden dimensions with learned gain/bias. It is widely used but can be slower than RMSNorm in some kernels.
Normalizes by root-mean-square without subtracting the mean, typically using only a learned gain. It can be more parameter- and compute-efficient while remaining stable.
Defines how logits are produced, including whether the head is untied, tied to embeddings, or structured to reduce parameters. This is a common place to free parameters under strict budgets.
Uses a separate weight matrix to map hidden states to vocabulary logits. This maximizes flexibility but can nearly double parameters when embeddings are also large.
Reuses the token embedding matrix as the output projection, reducing parameters and sometimes improving generalization. This is a high-leverage Parameter Golf trick.
Structures the output distribution to reduce compute and parameters for rare tokens. It can save parameters but adds complexity and may affect benchmark comparability.
Maximizes the likelihood of the next token under the model distribution. It is the standard objective for language modeling and typically parameter-free.
Blends the one-hot target with a uniform distribution to reduce overconfidence. This can improve generalization and calibration in small models.
Adds a penalty to stabilize logits and improve numerical behavior, often helpful with low precision. It can improve training stability without changing model size.
Represents the extra state stored per parameter (e.g., moments) and its precision. Under tight memory budgets, quantized or factorized states can free memory for larger batches or models.
Stores first and second moment estimates for each parameter, typically in FP32 for stability. This is effective but can dominate training memory for tiny models.
Quantizes optimizer moments to reduce memory while approximating Adam updates. This can significantly lower peak memory with modest accuracy impact when tuned.
Approximates second-moment statistics with factored representations to reduce memory. It is often used when optimizer-state memory is the main bottleneck.
Uses momentum buffers but avoids second-moment estimates, reducing state memory. It can be harder to tune for transformers but is memory-light.
Uses 32-bit floats for most tensors, offering stability at high memory cost. This often violates Parameter Golf memory constraints for longer contexts.
Uses half precision for activations/weights with selective FP32 accumulations. It is a standard approach to fit larger models/batches under memory limits.
Uses 8-bit floats for some tensors to further reduce memory and bandwidth. It can provide large savings but often needs careful scaling and kernel support.
Keeps forward activations for backpropagation, minimizing recomputation. This is compute-efficient but often hits memory ceilings first.
Recomputes some forward passes during backpropagation instead of storing all activations. This reduces peak memory and can unlock larger batch sizes in Parameter Golf.
Scores models under strict parameter and memory constraints, preventing trivial scaling. The benchmark incentivizes architectural and optimization innovations that fit tight budgets.
Computes model parameter count, optionally by component, to ensure compliance with size limits. It enables targeted parameter reallocation toward novel modules.
Estimates peak memory from activations, optimizer state, and KV cache to prevent exceeding constraints. It supports 'swap out' reasoning for memory-heavy components like attention.
